posts.name: D. HEIN
posts.cooked:
Great perspective, some of the many issues which make BORDEAUX wine valued!

Perspective of various issues and reviews!

TWA

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
An excellent read, and hopefully, a series he continues regularly, especially the 10 and 20 year reflections.

Love this quip, so sums up my views (and occasional debate with Leve):


By many metrics, Bordeaux’s vineyards have never been better maintained, nor Bordeaux’s wines better made. But if all great wines are made from ripe grapes, pristine ripeness and the absence of flaws do not of themselves constitute a compelling style.

posts.name: Br1an Th0rne
posts.cooked:
Love WK’s work, and am thrilled these “Up from the cellar” Bordeaux reviews will be a frequent occurrence. William is the primary reason I continue to subscribe to RP.com

William I would humbly request that you add 30 year vintage retrospectives to your schedule, for the interesting and exceptional years. The Bordeaux vintage that I cut my teeth on, 1995, is somehow approaching the 30 year mark!

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I was looking forward to this report, because William was the reason I went back to TWA, and I was definitely not disappointed.

There are large parts of it which I would like to copy and paste (larger than Robert’s quip!) but I will not because of copyright breach. I will just paraphrase one bit, concerning scores, where William makes clear that he intends to be less generous than other critics, in order to re-establish a clear quality hierarchy and to get away from the narrowing of the hierarchy and a dumbing-down of what it takes to be a “top wine” (here this is really my interpretation). I really could not agree more: architect-designed cellars, computer controlled plots and the usual consultants do not necessarily a great wine make.

The list of wines tasted includes three vintages of BAMA, which prompted me to check - and no, William was not the first to write a note - that honour goes to Neal Martin, who wrote about the 1970, but I seem to remember it was William’s bottle! Anyway, it’s a very eclectic collection of wines, which came as no surprise and I really look forward to reading more like this.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: And yet, the one thing that stood out to me was how generous the scores were. In almost every instance where I compared, his scores were equal to or higher than previous scores.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
And yet, the one thing that stood out to me was how generous the scores were. In almost every instance where I compared, his scores were equal to or higher than previous scores.

Given that these were bottles that were almost entirely drunk at home, from my cellar, there was a certain amount of preselection at work! In some cases I also marked up wines I consider underestimated (e.g. 02 Lynch Bages, 01 Léoville-Poyferré) for whatever reason.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Love WK’s work, and am thrilled these “Up from the cellar” Bordeaux reviews will be a frequent occurrence. William is the primary reason I continue to subscribe to RP.com

William I would humbly request that you add 30 year vintage retrospectives to your schedule, for the interesting and exceptional years. The Bordeaux vintage that I cut my teeth on, 1995, is somehow approaching the 30 year mark!

Thanks, Brian!

We’ll be going one better this year: 1982 at 40 years.

Lots of work to do right now getting a handle on current releases, but looking at mature wines is going to be a focus, of course.
